53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
177	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
178	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
179	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
180	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
181	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
182	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
184	
185	## Reading references
186	
187	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
188	
189	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
190	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
191	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
192	
193	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
194	
195	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
196	
197	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
198	
199	Conditions for dispatch:
200	
201	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
202	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
203	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
204	
205	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
206	
207	```
208	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
209	  --name reference-retriever \
210	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
211	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
212	  --write-domain 'references/**'
213	```
214	
215	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
216	
217	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
218	
219	## Workflow
220	
221	1. Read your directive completely.
222	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
223	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
224	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
225	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
226	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
227	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).

81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.

They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.

106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.

3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
80	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
81	$(\ast)$ as a single typeclass. The chapter pins $(*)$ to a \emph{layered}
82	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
83	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
84	
85	\begin{itemize}
86	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \Cref{def:prime_divisor} and
87	    \Cref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group $\Div(X)$, the degree
88	    \Cref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
89	    \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
90	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
91	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
92	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of $X$ already
93	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
94	    chain), so $X$ has a unique generic point and the function field
95	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
96	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
97	    the bare formal-sum data type.
98	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\Cref{def:order_at_point},
99	    \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, \Cref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
100	    signature additionally threads
101	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
102	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
103	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
104	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
105	    predicate ``for every $x \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1$ the
106	    stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
107	    the conjunction
108	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
109	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
110	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
111	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
112	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
113	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
114	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
115	    over $\bar k$ with explicit integrality witness, namely
116	    \begin{quote}
117	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
118	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
119	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
120	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
121	    \end{quote}
122	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
123	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
124	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is
125	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
126	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
127	    properness; integrality threaded).
128	\end{itemize}
129	
130	\noindent
131	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
132	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
133	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
134	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
135	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
136	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.

138	\section{Codim-$1$ cycle group}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
143	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
145	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
146	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
147	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
148	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
149	  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one."
150	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
151	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. A \emph{prime divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral
152	  subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
153	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
154	  Y$ be its generic point\ldots''), $Y$ is determined by its generic point
155	  $\eta \in X$: closed integral subschemes of codimension one of $X$ are in
156	  natural bijection with points $x \in X$ such that the closure
157	  $\overline{\{x\}} \subset X$ has codimension one. The closure
158	  $\overline{\{x\}}$, equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
159	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
160	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
161	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
162	
163	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
164	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
165	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
166	  the Mathlib predicate
167	  \[
168	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
169	  \]
170	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying $X$.
171	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
172	  ``$x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x$'' (i.e.\ $x \in \overline{\{y\}}$;
173	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
174	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
175	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
176	  preorder, so for the generic point $\eta_Y$ of a prime divisor $Y$ the
177	  only point strictly above $\eta_Y$ is the generic point $\eta_X$ of $X$,
178	  and the chain $\eta_Y < \eta_X$ has length one, giving
179	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta_Y$\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
180	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
181	  integral'' is automatic from $\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}$ being
182	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
183	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
184	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
185	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
186	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
187	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
188	
189	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
190	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
191	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
192	  \begin{itemize}
193	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
194	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
195	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
196	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
197	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
198	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
199	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
200	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
201	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
202	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
203	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
204	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
205	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
206	      uses.
207	  \end{itemize}
208	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
209	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
210	  needs.
211	\end{definition}
212	
213	\begin{definition}
214	
215	\leanok
216	[Codim-$1$ cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
217	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
219	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
220	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
221	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
222	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
223	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
224	  % group $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
225	  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
226	  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
227	  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
228	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
229	  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
230	  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
231	  \[
232	    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
233	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
234	  \]
235	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$
236	  (\Cref{def:prime_divisor}); an element $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely
237	  many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil
238	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all $n_i \geq 0$.
239	
240	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
241	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
242	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
243	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor $\to_0$ $\mathbb Z$}; integrality of $X$ is
244	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group $\Div(X)$ (it becomes
245	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
246	  field appears). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is the standing convention in the
247	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
248	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding'' above
249	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
250	\end{definition}

252	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
253	
254	\begin{definition}
255	
256	\leanok
257	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
258	  \label{def:order_at_point}
259	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
260	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
261	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
262	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
263	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
264	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
265	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
266	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
267	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
268	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
269	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
270	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
271	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
272	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
273	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
274	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
275	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
276	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
277	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
278	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
279	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
280	  \[
281	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
282	  \]
283	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
284	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
285	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
286	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
287	
288	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
289	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
290	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
291	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
292	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
293	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
294	\end{definition}

296	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
297	
298	\begin{definition}
299	
300	\leanok
301	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
302	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
304	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
305	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
306	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
307	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
308	  % be written $D = \sum n_i P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and
309	  % $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If $X$
311	  % is a nonsingular curve, then $X$ satisfies the condition $(\ast)$ used
312	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on $X$. A prime divisor is just
313	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
314	  % P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
315	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
316	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
317	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
318	  \[
319	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
320	  \]
321	  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
322	  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
323	  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
324	  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
325	  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
326	  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
327	
328	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
329	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
330	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a hypothesis
331	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}. It does \emph{not} typeclass-pin
332	  ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' at the carrier level; the
333	  well-definedness of \texttt{ofClosedPoint} as a \texttt{PrimeDivisor}
334	  promotion goes through a separate argument that, on a one-dimensional
335	  integral scheme, \texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} implies
336	  \texttt{Order.coheight P = 1}. The prover-agent threads the appropriate
337	  curve-level typeclasses inside the proof body, not in the signature; the
338	  blueprint-level statement is the one in the prose above.
339	\end{definition}

341	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
342	
343	\begin{definition}
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over $\bar k$]
347	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
349	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_closed_point}
350	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
351	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
352	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
353	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of $D$ to be $\sum n_i$."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
355	  nonsingular curve).}
356	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
357	  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
358	  \Div(C)$ is the integer
359	  \[
360	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
361	  \]
362	  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
363	  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
364	  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
365	  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
366	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
367	  $\bar k$ specialisation.
368	
369	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
370	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
371	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
372	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
373	  set ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
374	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
375	  $D \in \Div(X)$ on any scheme $X$ the integer-valued sum of its
376	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ this coincides
377	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
378	  residue field $\bar k$, so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
379	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
380	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
381	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
382	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
383	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
384	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
385	\end{definition}
386	
387	\begin{theorem}
388	
389	\leanok
390	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
391	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
393	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
394	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
395	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
396	  homomorphism
397	  \[
398	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
399	    \qquad
400	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
401	    \qquad
402	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
403	  \]
404	\end{theorem}
405	
406	\begin{proof}
407	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
408	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
409	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
410	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
411	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
412	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
413	\end{proof}

415	\section{Principal divisors}
416	
417	\begin{definition}
418	
419	\leanok
420	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
421	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
423	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
424	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
425	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
426	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
427	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
428	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
429	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
430	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
431	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
432	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
433	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
434	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
435	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
436	  p.~131.}
437	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
438	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
439	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
440	  \[
441	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
442	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
443	  \]
444	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
445	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
446	  the same definition specialises to
447	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
448	  sum over closed points.
449	\end{definition}
450	
451	\begin{theorem}
452	
453	\leanok
454	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
455	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
456	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
457	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
458	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
459	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
460	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
461	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
462	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
463	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
464	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
465	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
466	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
467	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
468	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
469	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
470	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
471	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
472	  \[
473	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
474	    \qquad
475	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
476	    \qquad
477	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
478	  \]
479	\end{theorem}
480	
481	\begin{proof}
482	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
483	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
484	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
485	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
486	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
487	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
488	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
489	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
490	  identities.
491	\end{proof}
492	
493	\begin{theorem}
494	
495	\leanok
496	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
497	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
498	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
499	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
500	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
501	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
502	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
503	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
504	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
505	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
506	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
507	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
508	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
509	  $\bar k$. For every nonzero rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ the
510	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
511	  \[
512	    \deg(\div(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
513	  \]
514	\end{theorem}
515	
516	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let $f \in K(X)^\ast$. If $f \in k$, then
517	% $(f) = 0$, so there is nothing to prove. If $f \notin k$, then the
518	% inclusion of fields $k(f) \subset K(X)$ induces a finite morphism
519	% $\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1$. It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
520	% have $(f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})$. Since $\{0\} - \{\infty\}$
521	% is a divisor of degree 0 on $\mathbf P^1$, we conclude that $(f)$ has
522	% degree 0 on $X$."
523	\begin{proof}
524	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
525	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If $f \in \bar k \subset K(C)$ is
526	  a constant scalar, then $f$ has no zeros or poles, so $\div(f) = 0$ and
527	  $\deg(\div(f)) = 0$. Otherwise the inclusion of fields
528	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ exhibits $K(C)$ as a finite extension of the
529	  rational function field $\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)$, so the corresponding
530	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is finite (by the
531	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
532	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
533	  divisor $\div(f)$ equals the pullback $\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])$
534	  along $\varphi$ of the degree-$0$ divisor $[0] - [\infty]$ on
535	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$; since pullback along a finite morphism
536	  multiplies degrees by $\deg(\varphi)$, we conclude
537	  $\deg(\div(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
538	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
539	
540	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
541	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ defines a finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$''
542	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
543	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
544	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
545	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
546	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
547	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
548	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
549	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
550	\end{proof}

552	\section{Divisor class group}
553	
554	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
555	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
556	the \emph{Picard group} of $C$ in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
557	interpretation $\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)$ requires the construction of
558	$\mathcal O_C(D)$, which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
559	out of scope here.
560	
561	\begin{definition}
562	
563	\leanok
564	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
565	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
567	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
568	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
569	  % and of $\operatorname{Cl} X$) (read from
570	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
571	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. Two divisors $D$
572	  % and $D'$ are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$,
573	  % if $D - D'$ is a principal divisor. The group $\operatorname{Div} X$ of
574	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
575	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of $X$, and is denoted by
576	  % $\operatorname{Cl} X$."
577	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
578	  $\operatorname{Cl} X$).}
579	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. Two Weil divisors $D, D' \in \Div(X)$ are
580	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$, if and only if there
581	  exists $f \in K(X)^{\times}$ with
582	  \[
583	    D - D' \;=\; \div(f) \;\in\; \Div(X).
584	  \]
585	  The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $\Div(X)$ (the
586	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
587	  identities $\div(1) = 0$, $\div(f^{-1}) = -\div(f)$, $\div(fg) =
588	  \div(f) + \div(g)$ via \Cref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
589	  quotient group $\Cl(X) := \Div(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)$ is the
590	  \emph{divisor class group} of $X$. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over
591	  $\bar k$, \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
592	  $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ descends to a homomorphism
593	  $\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z$.
594	\end{definition}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, $k$ denotes a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ of relative dimension $1$. In other words, $C \to \Spec k$ is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$; see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$ is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the $k$-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of $C$.
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-$k$-modules construction and applying the $k$-linear sheaf cohomology $H^1$. The result is a $k$-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's $\ModuleCat k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier $\toModuleKSheaf C$ is the sheaf-of-$k$-modules constructed from $\struct C$ via the structure morphism; the wrapper $\HModule k F 1$ is the $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$, which carries a canonical $\Module k$-structure inherited from the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ returns the $k$-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme guarantees $\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty$, so $g(C)$ is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})$ (Serre duality).
45	  \item $g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)$, where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over $\mathbb C$, $g(C)$ equals the topological genus of $C(\mathbb C)$.
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition $\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ requires a $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of $\struct C$ on the topology of opens of the underlying space of $C$. Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\AddCommGrpCat$-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)$.
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel $\Module k$-valued construction with unit object $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$ instead of $\ULift\,\mathbb Z$, gluing through the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a $\Module k$ structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaf} (this project): for $C$ over $\Spec k$, the sheafification of the presheaf $U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))$ where the $k$-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{$H^0$ algebraic bridge} (this project): $H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)$, via Mathlib's $\Ext$ linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content $H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k$ on a connected proper $k$-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for $H^i(C, \mathcal F)$ on a proper $k$-curve.} Mathlib's $\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}$ provides $\Module.\mathtt{Finite}$ for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes $\mathtt{def\ genus}$, not $\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}$. Because $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$, $\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}$, and the structure-sheaf-as-$k$-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the $\mathtt{noncomputable}$ modifier on the $\mathtt{def}$. The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem $\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}$ in $\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}$ states that the Jacobian of $C$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$ --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.

103	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}` — On a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}` [expected]. 104	4. `\theorem` `\label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}` — Genus-0 specialisation: on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$ and $D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)$ with $\deg(D) \geq 0$, $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}` [expected]. 106	**`\uses` skeleton**:

108	- `def:l_invariant` uses `def:codim1_cycles` (from `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex`), `def:eulerChar_curve`.

111	
112	**Main theorem proof strategy**: Riemann–Roch for genus 0 is the case where Serre duality collapses (the canonical divisor $K_C$ has $\deg K_C = -2 < 0$ on $\mathbb P^1$, so $\ell(K_C - D) = 0$ for $\deg D \geq 0$). Hartshorne IV Thm 1.3 derives the full RR via the additive sub-divisor $D \mapsto D + P$ inductive step on $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) - \deg(D)$; the genus-0 specialisation is one short corollary. The induction step requires the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + P) \to \mathcal O_C(D + P)|_P \to 0$ and additivity of $\chi$ in short exact sequences (Mathlib: `CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar_additive` — present). The Serre-duality-free formulation avoids the Mathlib dualizing-sheaf gap.

117	- `analogies/rrbridge-survey.md` (in-tree) — the project's existing RR-bridge survey.

Recommendation: **(ii) direct $\chi$** — Mathlib has no dualizing sheaf, and the inductive proof needs only short-exact-sequence additivity of $\chi$, a project asset already partially present in `Cohomology_*.tex`.
